findings.message: No helmet() call detected in this file ΓÇö verify security headers are set.
findings.why: Without secure headers, apps are more vulnerable to XSS, clickjacking, and MIME sniffing.
findings.fix: Use helmet() at app bootstrap or equivalent header middleware.

findings.message: CORS configured with origin: '*' ΓÇö any site can read responses.
findings.why: Wildcard origin removes browser same-origin protection for credentialed or sensitive responses.
findings.fix: Set origin to explicit allowlist or a function that validates the Origin header.

findings.message: Missing auth middleware on sensitive route POST /api/login
findings.why: State-changing endpoints on login, registration, upload, webhook, or similar surfaces without recognizable authentication are high-risk for abuse.
findings.fix: Apply authentication middleware (e.g. JWT verify, passport) before the handler.
findings.remediation: Apply authentication middleware (e.g. JWT verify, passport) before the handler where appropriate.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/login
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Sensitive path /api/login lacks rate limiting
findings.why: Auth, upload, webhook, and messaging endpoints are abuse targets without throttling.
findings.fix: Add express-rate-limit or similar on this route.

findings.message: SQL built with string concatenation or template literals can lead to SQL injection.
findings.why: Untrusted input concatenated into a SQL string can break out of the query and execute arbitrary SQL.
findings.fix: Use parameterized queries, e.g. db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id]), or an ORM with parameter binding.

findings.message: SQL Injection risk
findings.why: Untrusted input flows into a SQL query via string concatenation.
findings.fix: Use parameterized queries: db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?", [id])

findings.message: Missing auth on sensitive admin/mod route POST /admin/roles
findings.why: Admin, moderation, and abuse-handling routes must require authentication and authorization.
findings.fix: Require authentication and role checks (e.g. admin-only middleware) before this handler.
findings.remediation: Require authentication and role checks (e.g. admin-only middleware) before this handler.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /admin/roles
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Missing auth middleware on sensitive route POST /webhooks/stripe
findings.why: State-changing endpoints on login, registration, upload, webhook, or similar surfaces without recognizable authentication are high-risk for abuse.
findings.fix: Apply authentication middleware (e.g. JWT verify, passport) before the handler.
findings.remediation: Apply authentication middleware (e.g. JWT verify, passport) before the handler where appropriate.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /webhooks/stripe
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Sensitive path /webhooks/stripe lacks rate limiting
findings.why: Auth, upload, webhook, and messaging endpoints are abuse targets without throttling.
findings.fix: Add express-rate-limit or similar on this route.

findings.message: Webhook-style route POST /webhooks/stripe may lack signature verification
findings.why: Payment and integration webhooks should validate HMAC or provider signatures before trusting the body.
findings.fix: Verify provider signatures (e.g. Stripe constructEvent) using the raw body and secret; use timing-safe comparison.
findings.remediation: Verify provider signatures (e.g. Stripe constructEvent) using the raw body and secret; use timing-safe comparison.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/run
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Shell command built from string concatenation or template literals can lead to command injection.
findings.why: User-controlled input in a shell command string can escape and run arbitrary commands.
findings.fix: Use execFile with an array of arguments, or strictly sanitize input.

findings.message: SQL built with string concatenation or template literals can lead to SQL injection.
findings.why: Untrusted input concatenated into a SQL string can break out of the query and execute arbitrary SQL.
findings.fix: Use parameterized queries, e.g. db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id]), or an ORM with parameter binding.

findings.message: SQL Injection risk
findings.why: Untrusted input flows into a SQL query via string concatenation.
findings.fix: Use parameterized queries: db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?", [id])

findings.message: File path may be derived from user input, enabling path traversal.
findings.why: If the path comes from req.query, req.params, or similar, an attacker can use '../' to read or write outside intended directories.
findings.fix: Resolve paths against a fixed base directory and normalize, e.g. path.join(BASE_DIR, path.normalize(userPath)). Do not use user input directly as a path.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/dyn
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: eval() and similar enable code injection from untrusted input.
findings.why: Dynamic code execution from a string allows an attacker to run arbitrary code if the string is user-controlled or derived from user input.
findings.fix: Use JSON.parse() for data, or a safe data structure. Do not pass user input or concatenated strings into eval, Function(), or setTimeout(string).

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/digest
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Avoid weak hash algorithm 'md5'. For password storage use a password hashing function; for general hashing use SHA-256+.
findings.why: MD5 and SHA-1 are cryptographically broken. If this hashes passwords, they are vulnerable to brute-force. For integrity/signatures use SHA-256 or SHA-3.
findings.fix: For passwords: use bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, or argon2 with salting (e.g. bcrypt.hashSync(password, 10)). For general hashing: crypto.createHash('sha256') or crypto.createHash('sha512').

findings.message: Math.random() must not be used for security-sensitive values (tokens, keys, nonces, salts).
findings.why: Math.random() is not cryptographically secure. Predictable values can be guessed and lead to token forgery or key recovery.
findings.fix: Use crypto.randomBytes(n) or crypto.getRandomValues() for tokens, keys, IVs, nonces, and salts.

findings.message: Possible hardcoded secret in source.
findings.why: Secrets in code get committed to version control and can be leaked. They should not live in source.
findings.fix: Use environment variables (process.env.SECRET_KEY) or a secrets manager. Never commit real secrets.

findings.message: Insecure env var fallback ΓÇö if env var unset, hardcoded secret is used
findings.why: If the app runs without the env var set, a guessable default may be used in production.
findings.fix: Remove fallback. Throw error if env var is missing at startup.
findings.remediation: Remove fallback. Throw error if env var is missing at startup.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/jwt-issue
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: JWT signed with a known weak secret literal.
findings.why: Attackers who guess or know this secret can forge tokens.
findings.fix: Use a long random secret from a vault or env; never use dictionary/example strings.
findings.remediation: Replace with a high-entropy secret loaded only from secure configuration.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/jwt-check
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: JWT verify() uses a known weak secret literal.
findings.why: Weak JWT verification secrets can be guessed, allowing forged tokens to pass verification.
findings.fix: Load a high-entropy verification secret from secure configuration (vault/KMS/env), never dictionary literals.
findings.remediation: Replace weak verify secret with a long random key and rotate credentials.

findings.message: Disabling TLS certificate verification (rejectUnauthorized: false) allows man-in-the-middle attacks.
findings.why: With verification disabled, an attacker can intercept and modify traffic. Only use for local debugging, never in production.
findings.fix: Remove rejectUnauthorized: false. Use proper CA certificates and fix certificate issues instead of disabling verification.

findings.message: ip.isPublic() is an insufficient SSRF defense ΓÇö bypassed by octal/hex/IPv6 IP forms
findings.why: The ip package misclassified several non-canonical IP shapes (CVEs below); do not rely on it as the sole SSRF control.
findings.fix: Use an explicit allowlist of expected hostnames instead of IP classification.
findings.remediation: Use an explicit allowlist of expected hostnames instead of IP classification

findings.message: SSRF risk: untrusted data reaches outbound request URL.
findings.why: Attackers can make the server request internal or cloud metadata endpoints.
findings.fix: Use an allowlist of hosts/schemes; block private IPs and link-local addresses.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/merge-profile
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Potential prototype pollution via unsafe deep merge / set with user-controlled input.
findings.why: Merging attacker-controlled keys like __proto__ or constructor.prototype can mutate object prototypes globally.
findings.fix: Validate/strip dangerous keys (__proto__, constructor, prototype), use safe merge libraries, and reject nested prototype paths.
findings.remediation: Block prototype keys before merge/set and avoid deep merge on untrusted objects.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/deep-set
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Prototype pollution risk: user input in deep merge or path set.
findings.why: Attacker-controlled objects can pollute Object.prototype.
findings.fix: Avoid merging user input into objects with deep merge; validate keys; use Object.create(null).

findings.message: Potential prototype pollution via unsafe deep merge / set with user-controlled input.
findings.why: Merging attacker-controlled keys like __proto__ or constructor.prototype can mutate object prototypes globally.
findings.fix: Validate/strip dangerous keys (__proto__, constructor, prototype), use safe merge libraries, and reject nested prototype paths.
findings.remediation: Block prototype keys before merge/set and avoid deep merge on untrusted objects.

findings.message: Missing CSRF protection on POST /api/log
findings.why: Cross-site requests can trigger unwanted state changes without a CSRF token or SameSite strategy.
findings.fix: Use csrf/csurf/doubleCsrf or strict SameSite cookies for session auth.

findings.message: Log message built from user input without sanitization can lead to log injection.
findings.why: Unsanitized input in logs lets attackers insert fake entries, hide actions, or impersonate others via CR/LF and control characters.
findings.fix: Sanitize log input: strip or escape carriage return and line feed. Limit log entry size. Do not pass raw user input as the log message.

findings.message: Log injection risk
findings.why: Unsanitized input in logs can allow fake entries or impersonation via CR/LF.
findings.fix: Sanitize log input: strip CR/LF; limit message size.
